data.title: The Art Of Profitable Scale: Lessons From Progressive
data.description:
Several years ago, I began analyzing The Progressive Corporation (PGR), one of the most transparent property and casualty (P&C) insurance carriers I am familiar with, which publishes monthly, detailed results. From the outset, I was impressed by the straightforward business model, the rigorous underwriting discipline, the effective cash flow generation, and the steadily increasing revenues and income. In my latest article covering PGR, I compared the insurer with one of its peers, GEICO, without Warren Buffett's magic touch regarding the investment allocation.

I do still believe that Progressive is a twin of GEICO, following a similar cost steering strategy to overperform, acquire new customers, and continue growing at a rapid pace.

The recent May results have amazed me again. The motor insurer has continued delivering steady results in both motor and homeowners' insurance businesses, despite the catastrophe costs and the elevated inflation.

Underwriting Discipline: A Masterclass in Pricing and Profitability

Chubb (CB) is generally known as one of the best-in-class P&C insurers in the U.S. Over the last ten years, the Evan Greenberg-managed insurer has had a 10-year average combined ratio of 89.8%, vs. a 97.8% of its peers.

Although Progressive's underwriting performance is less impressive than Chubb's, PGR is present in a challenging and highly competitive market. Hence, the over-cycle underwriting performance of the motor insurer remains astonishing, as many motor insurers struggle to deliver a combined ratio below 100%.

For those who are familiar with the concept of "combined ratio", it is the key metric for P&C insurers and could be defined as follows:

The combined ratio, also called "the combined ratio after policyholder dividends ratio," is a measure of profitability used by an insurance company to gauge how well it is performing in its daily operations. The combined ratio is calculated by taking the sum of incurred losses and expenses and then dividing them by the earned premium.

In Progressive's case, the 10-year combined ratio trend was the following:

Year Combined Ratio
2024 88.8%
2023 93.3%
2022 95.8%
2021 95.3%
2020 87.8%
2019 90.9%
2018 89.5%
2017 92.6%
2016 95.1%
2015 92.5%
2014 92.3%

With a 10-year average of 92.2%, the motor insurance carrier delivered steady underwriting performance. Those strong underwriting margins were coupled with a consistent double-digit premium growth. Progressive's gross written premiums increased from $19.6 billion in 2014 to $65.5 billion in 2024.

Consequently, the insurer succeeded in delivering steady and gradually increasing underwriting results over the cycle, despite the increasing claim inflation, the volatility in the claims experience, and the intensive pricing pressure.

The 2024 underwriting income and margins were exceptionally high, with an underwriting margin of 11.2% and an almost double underwriting gain compared to FY2023.

In 2025, the motor insurance carrier continued to deliver steady underwriting results. For the first five months of the year, Progressive reported an 86% combined ratio, while the net earned premiums amounted to circa $32.8 billion, or an underwriting income of $4.5 billion pre-tax. Last year in the same period, the underwriting income was about $2.8 billion pre-tax, with earned premiums of $27.6 billion, and a combined ratio of 89.7%.

Progressive has started 2025 on the right foot. Some of us would add "as always" or "as expected". I do believe that the secret ingredient of Progressive's success is how the insurer succeeds in steering its expense ratio.

Progressive's Expense Ratio: A Game Changer

Generally, the insurers used to have an expense ratio oscillating between 30% and 35%. On the other hand, specialty and niche insurers often operate with expense ratios well above the 35% threshold. This is largely driven by the need to pay higher commissions to intermediaries, along with the structural characteristics of their business models.

These insurers typically focus on specific risks or high-net-worth individuals, and their elevated expense ratios reflect:

  • Lower premium volumes, which limit operating leverage
  • Specialized underwriting processes requiring expert talent
  • Higher acquisition costs, especially in broker-driven channels

For example, Old Republic's (ORI) title division has an extremely high expense ratio (above 90%). In 2022, 2023, and 2024, the commissions represented from 68 to 70% of the earned premiums.

Another specialty insurer, Palomar (PLMR), used to record an expense ratio above 50% for a total combined ratio below 80%.

Those insurers remained profitable because their claims experience tends to be more favorable, with lower frequency or severity, keeping the loss ratio under tighter control.

For Progressive, it is a different story. It is a mass-market insurer, competing with local, regional, and national peers. Furthermore, the customers (also called the policyholders) are sensitive to the price they will pay for being covered. This pricing pressure results in intense competition across the insurance carriers. Therefore, the underwriting margin is closely linked to the cost-controlling process. The more Progressive succeeds in reducing or controlling external and internal costs, the higher the underwriting margin will be.

And the insurance carrier succeeds in having an expense ratio below 20%, which is impressive. From 2019 to 2024, the underwriting expense ratio oscillated between 19.6% and 23.7%.

The peak observed in 2020 was related to premium refunds due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Although the 2020 expense ratio was above the average, the metric performance remains amazing. The few similar examples I have in mind are not in the U.S., but in Nordic countries, where insurers are consistently monitoring their costs to deliver as high as possible underwriting returns.

The Property Business: Small But Improving Over The Years

Another ingredient to Progressive's success is the property business. This segment has remained small over the year compared to the motor business, but Progressive succeeded in expanding its footprint in the U.S. with an aggressive commercial expansion. Expanding business into property insurance segments offered multiple advantages: (1) client retention increased; (2) it increased the segment diversification, impacting the loss ratio level, and (3) the additional earned premiums fueled the investment portfolio, generating additional financial income.

However, it was not always as simple as I mentioned before. The property business is also highly sensitive to catastrophe losses, and Progressive's property business is regularly affected by natural disasters, affecting the loss ratio and the underwriting margins accordingly. From 2019 to 2024, the property's combined ratio was as follows:

Year Combined Ratio
2019 101.7%
2020 107.1%
2021 115.3%
2022 110.5%
2023 98.9%
2024 98.3%

Since 2023, the property business has become profitable. In 05M2025, the insurance carrier recorded a combined ratio of 87.4% for the property business, vs. a 137.1% combined ratio in 05M2024.

Going forward, the underwriting performance of Progressive's homeowner insurance segment might be lower as this business could be massively affected by catastrophic events, like those in Florida or California. Even though Progressive succeeds in meeting an underwriting margin of only 2% (or a combined ratio of 98%) over the cycle, the benefits of being present in this massive but highly competitive market will be there.

Valuation: You Pay High Quality Coupled With Double-Digit Growth

The first time I analyzed Progressive, I considered it expensive. I miscalculated, or to be more accurate, I fooled myself by considering that the expected growth was unsustainable. Figures have proven to me, I was wrong, and the stock performance over the last five years was astonishing. Over the last five years, the company's total return was circa 271%. Comparatively, S&P 500 returned 120.2%.

Is this bull run well-deserved? I do believe so. The company has proven to investors it was well-managed, growth-focused, without sacrificing growth over margins. The company also benefited from higher rates, fueling the investment portfolio, which generated $2.8 billion of revenues in 2024 and is expected to generate around $3.3 billion in 2025. The stock performance from the last five years is the consequence of the decisions made by Progressive's executive management team.

When you invest in Progressive, you expect the company to grow at a double-digit pace, with steady and reliable underwriting margins, coupled with increasing investment income. For some investors, it might be boring: no AI, no digital disruption, no crypto. Others consider Progressive remains an interesting choice in terms of investment picking. And they are even willing to pay 4.8 times the company's book value and above 19 times the earnings.

Based on the P/B metric, Progressive is not cheap. But it never was. By looking at P/E, it is a different story, although it is highly dependent on future growth. As of now, investors may buy a piece of Progressive for 17.8 times the earnings.

Comparatively, Travelers (TRV), Allstate (ALL), or Chubb trade at around 14 times the earnings.

Picky and greedy investors could argue that the PE premium is not deserved. I do believe the opposite. Even though Progressive's underwriting performance is less impressive than Chubb's, the motor insurer grows faster. When you invest in Progressive, you are for profitable growth. And luckily and hopefully, you get it.

Final Thoughts

Years passed, and I remain impressed. I remain impressed by the premium growth, the company's transformation, underwriting performance, and investment portfolio growth.

I missed the train some years ago. I could regret it, but the past is past, and we all make mistakes when we invest (spoiler alert, I will certainly make others). I don't know when the growth will stop (maybe, never?), but Progressive remains one of the most brilliant and well-managed insurers I have analyzed. I am not a shareholder of the company, but I would love to be. Although the current safety margin is minimal (potentially 8% based on the P/E metric), I remain tempted to invest in Progressive, to be part of this amazing business story.

data.title: The Market Is Too Excited About Oracle
data.description:
Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) is continuing to hit all-time highs with a market cap approaching $600 billion as the company's enterprise business ties in with the enormous opportunity within artificial intelligence. The company is benefiting from its growing cloud revenue; however, we think the market has gotten ahead of itself, and it's now overvalued.

Oracle 2025 Fiscal Year Results

Oracle had relatively strong 2025 fiscal results, with 41% growth in performance obligations ($138 billion in potential future revenue).

The company's annualized revenue grew past $60 billion with 115 YoY, supported by strong growth (27%) in the company's cloud revenue to $27 billion. Overall revenue from the year grew by 8% and the company's GAAP earnings per share of $4.8 annualized put it at a P/E of ~50, one that requires future growth but isn't exceedingly high.

Oracle 2026 Guidance and Cloud

Oracle expects its cloud growth rates to increase substantially in 2026 as demand for artificial intelligence-based datacenters increases.

Starting from an already lofty positioning, that's quite impressive for the company. The company is integrating strongly with other cloud providers including Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Azure, and it's building a number of datacenters. However, it's also worth noting the company is heavily dependent on these hyperscalers, many of which have their own database offerings.

We expect these companies to increasingly push clients to use their own database offerings rather than using Oracle.

Oracle Specific Financial Picture

At the end of the day, what matters is financials and the growth potential.

Oracle is spending heavily on datacenters, $10 billion in 2025 alone, as the company contends that demand remains quite high. However, that substantial spending comes as a long-term bet in an increasingly competitive market, where other companies are also spending massively. That could lead to a pricing war to sustain demand for these datacenters, hurting margins.

The company's annualized revenue is more than $60 billion, meaning the company is trading at a P/S of ~10x and a P/E of ~50x, as margins for the company have remained quite high. The vast majority of the company's revenue and a growing portion comes from cloud services. However, we also see competition here as other cloud providers have their own offerings.

At the same time, we expect the crazy AI wave to slow down. It depends on who you ask, but there are already some indications that the rate of datacenter demand growth is slowing down. That's key because many AI companies have valuations dependent on double-digit growth going forward.

We expect as the free for all where companies risk being left behind slows, companies will be more pragmatic about where they're spending massive amounts of capital. That will result in companies pushing for pricing benefits and increased competition between the major cloud providers who risk overbuilding data centers. All of that could hurt margins.

Oracle Shareholder Returns

Oracle used to have a strong history of buybacks, to the extent where you could find commentary about the company's use of debt to fuel buybacks.

In hindsight, due to the timing with the artificial intelligence wave, it appears to have been the correct decision for the company. Despite that, the company has slowed down the pace of buybacks substantially. One could argue that the company now has opportunities to deploy capital into growth areas, and the company's higher share price makes buybacks less ideal.

However, it also shows the effect of the company's share-based compensation, something which isn't traditionally marked as an expense. That 2% annualized dilutive impact is $12 billion, or roughly 80% of the company's annual earnings. That's a real cost as shareholders are diluted, even if it's not an obvious one.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the current growth era that the company is in. As long as companies are focused on growing no matter what, the company could see its revenues and potential profits grow substantially. That could make the company a valuable investment opportunity that can drive the returns to justify its valuation.

Conclusion

Oracle is a great company at the right time. The company's enterprise positioning and strong technological portfolio means that as companies chase artificial intelligence and organizations fear being left behind, the company is incredibly well positioned. Despite that, valuation matters and even for the best companies in the world, there's a valuation to pause and think at.

At its current valuation, especially with competitive threats and the potential for slowing demand, massive capital spending, and the cash impact of shareholder dilution, we think the company is a poor investment. As a result, we recommend against investing in the company at the current time. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Daikin Industries: Poor Earnings Growth Making Lower Lows Likely (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

We initially began covering Daikin (DKILF) (OTCPK:DKILY) in October of last year and then followed up with fresh commentary in January of this year. In both articles, we rated the air-conditioning and refrigeration manufacturer a 'Hold' but recommended caution due to both fundamental & technical concerns. Shares are now down over 22% since our initial commentary last October.

From a fundamental standpoint, although revenues have increased in recent times, sustained margin pressure has meant that bottom-line profitability has not returned to historic averages, which investors have become accustomed to.

In the company's most recent fiscal year numbers (announced May 8, 2025), Daikin announced growth in net sales and operating profit compared to the previous year. However, given that Daikin is a multi-billion-dcaollar multinational company, it derives its revenues from multiple international markets in local currencies. As the company is Japanese, these sales every year are ultimately reported in yen when converted to Daikin's home currency.

As we see below, although Daikin reported an operating profit gain in its most recent fiscal year, if we exclude the forex tailwind over the past 12 months, annual EBIT fell for the Japanese outfit.

This trend revisits what we have been stating about Daikin all along, in that its products outside Japan continue to get more expensive in real terms, all things remaining equal. Yes, revenue growth has bought the company some time, but sluggish demand in the likes of Housing Ducted Unitary in the Americas & Heat Pumps in Europe continues to weigh on Daikin's numbers. The much smaller Chemical business has also been impacted by sluggish semiconductor demand in recent quarters.

Therefore, let's dig into Daikin's technical charts to see if investors are beginning to price in a turnaround for the Japanese Air conditioner manufacturer or the established downtrend is set to continue.

Long-Term Technicals

The benefits of using long-term charting for trend analysis and forecasting cannot be understated. Multi-decade charts, for example, can compress share-price action, enabling the analysis of much longer periods. Furthermore, given investors want to always position themselves in alignment with the dominant trend, it makes sense to consult longer periods & associated perspectives.

As chartists, we believe that all known fundamentals have been completely embedded in Daikin's share-price action. Therefore, by studying the resulting 'effects' of how participating investors view DKILF, we can quickly extrapolate the state of the company's fundamentals up to this point.

The three concerning events that are evident on Daikin's long-term technicals are

  1. The overbought MACD bearish crossover in early 2022. MACD crossovers are particularly noteworthy on long-term charts due to the dual role of the indicator (Momentum & Trend) & the amount of information that makes up the signals.
  2. In mid to late 2022, shares registered a long-term death cross (crossing of Daikin's 10-month average below its 40-month counterpart), giving weight to the previous MACD crossover. The bearish crossover took place at practically the same time as the breach of Daikin's multi-year upward trendline (depicted below)
  3. Since 2024, the ADX trend-following indicator has been rising, demonstrating that the downtrend has indeed been strengthening. The worrying aspect here for the bulls is that the long-term ADX reading still only stands at 25, despite the significant losses in recent years. Regarding the functioning of the ADX, the higher this trend-following rises (from a low starting point, which is what we have), the stronger the probability that the established trend will continue.

Intermediate 5-Year Technicals

Although descending triangles usually appear as a continuation pattern on technical charts, it appears that this bearish pattern may have been used to print a multi-year market top in Daikin. As we see below, Daikin topped out in August 2021 above $26 a share and then went out to print lower highs & lower lows with support holding just above the $14 level on multiple occasions. When support fell in earnest in 2024, this prompted the multi-year bearish triangle was about to play itself out. Given the height of the triangle is over $12, this has bearish connotations if indeed this triangle plays itself out in full. Either way, you feel, it will take a monumental effort for shares to trade above the $14 to $15 level for a prolonged period any time soon.

12-Month Technical Chart

When we go to Daikin's short-term technicals, although shares have managed to rally off their early April lows, we do not see any sustained up-move gaining traction for the following reason.

Firstly, the rally (which printed the May highs) stopped right at overhead resistance just above $12 a share. Since then, shares have not been able to regain this level with either a pennant or symmetrical triangle (both being likely continuation patterns to the downside), most likely playing itself out at present.

Bulls may negate the above premise, but there continues to be insufficient buying volume in our eyes to sustain a strong upmove above $12 a share. Nevertheless, even if we are incorrect on our assumption, the next significant resistance level presents itself above the $14 level, as we have learned on the intermediate chart earlier.

Therefore, the question to be asked here is whether the risk justifies the reward from a bullish standpoint in Daikin at this time? We deem not where one solely has to look at the first chart above to see the amount of white-space below the prevailing share price.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, given Daikin's growing margin problems, stemming from weak demand in multiple markets, we are downgrading our rating in DKILF to a 'Sell' until further notice. The company's technicals continue to reflect bearish sentiment, with the long-term bearish trend gaining downward traction in recent quarters. We look forward to continued coverage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Repay Holdings: Undervalued With Upcoming Growth Surge
data.description:
EVP - Momentum

If I were to trade solely on Repay Holdings' (NASDAQ:RPAY) stagnant share price this year, I would be blind to a nascent inflection that is quietly underway. The stock currently trades at just 2.47x EV/Sales - a staggering 57% discount to its own historical average - even as product adoption, software integrations, and strategy momentum are accelerating largely under the radar. Meanwhile, investor attention remains trapped on headline growth deceleration in '24, while actual (bottom-up) momentum is strong as my record onboarding of 14 new credit unions and a pipeline of 283+ software integrations last quarter are laying the foundation for a substantial ramp.

As a primer, Repay is a provider of modern payment processing technology (digital card, ACH, instant funding) that enables seamless payments (web, mobile App, text/SMS) in underserved and niche verticals, such as: Auto & Personal Lending, B2B Payments, and Receivables Management. Repay's technology has been designed to simplify complex payment flows for both lenders and borrowers. In short, it simplifies payments for lenders. The reason I am pounding the table today: My analysis indicates 120% equity upside and a $10 PT, based on a 3.5x FY26 EV/Sales multiple that I believe better reflects the near-term leverage headwind.

First, enterprise sales investments are starting to pay off as evidenced by large client wins as well as the DD ramp in Instant Funding Volumes (Instant Funding volumes grew +19% y/y in Q1).

Second, the post SBC FCF yield of 14% is cheap, signaling strong operating leverage despite a $220M convertible note coming due in early '26.

Third, a formal strategic review in conjunction with significant insider buying points to value realization in some form, be it bolt-on M&A, go-private offer, or lucrative commercial partnership.

I am quick to note RPAY's heightened net leverage and near-term customer turnover as key risks. While leverage is elevated, the balance sheet is clean, and my estimates already incorporate muted rev growth (3.5%) in FY25 before a meaningful reacceleration in FY26. In my view, RPAY represents a classic case of 'ignore the noise, buy the set-up'. Once integration momentum and new wins filter through financials, I do not expect it will be long before a re-rating occurs. For patient investors, I believe the current valuation is a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. My sense is that RPAY moves up quickly from here, and I am a Strong Buy.

Software integrations boom

Fundamentally, I believe the power of REPAY’s expanding the cadence of software integrations is underappreciated. My confidence stems from both cadence as well as strategic value. With 283+ integrations across a variety of vertical-focused platforms, I note the strategic value of each new partnership is not just an expansion of the potential funnel for Repay, but firming of integration into the workflows of credit unions, independent merchants, and enterprise business clients. In the last reported quarter, Repay announced two new software integrations, and added 14 new credit unions for a total base in the segment of 343.

The crux of my thesis is that the expanding integration base delivers a compounding effect. Specifically, every new software integration potentially opens door to dozens or hundreds of downstream clients already using the software, providing Repay with a spectrum of flows to monetize with minimal incremental sales effort. Complete network effects in my view, and certainly no small effect in the fragmented markets in which Repay plays (I believe vertical expertise and connectivity commands premium pricing and stay-in power in these markets).

I see evidence of this translating to pipeline materiality. Management continues to speak about a robust sales pipeline directly tied to integrations, and implementation timelines that are actively being compressed as technical overlaps multiply. That's a far cry from the labor-intensive enterprise by enterprise sales cycles of REA's past maturity. In my view, an accelerating cadence of software integrations is the most important leading catalyst reflected in REPAY's forecast for FY26E re-acceleration in revenue growth. I see this dynamic underappreciated at current levels, and recommend overweight.

Measured Re-Acceleration

I expect Repay Holdings to deliver FY25E revenue growth of 3.5% y/y over FY24, followed by a notable re-acceleration of 11.8% y/y to $362.1mn in FY26E on a variety of tangible catalysts that support my confidence in estimates. My channel checks into enterprise-wide sales indicate that investments are beginning to bear fruit, supported by new wins and a re-acceleration in implementation rates. Strong onboarding momentum, for example 14 new credit unions in Q1 and more than 282 software integrations, increases reach and leads to direct growth in payments volume, while Instant Funding is starting to show signs of leverage as volumes grow 19.0% y/y and management believes it can expand its use to new verticals.

The consensus assumes 8.0% revenue growth in FY25E, though my forecast anticipates 3.5% y/y growth as Q1 revenue weakness rolls through the year and I conservatively recognize client churn. Heading into FY26E, I see reasons for growing optimism as integration momentum and product adoption kick off a re-acceleration in growth. I remain cautious on macro and regulatory risk but see a healthy growth driver underneath and believe investors should look for a clear earnings inflection by late FY25E.

Discounted EV/Sales Drives My Framework

I arrive at my $10 RPAY PT using a 3.5x EV/Sales multiple applied to my FY26E revenue forecast of $362.1mn. This disciplined valuation framework compares to the 5.74x long-term average, reflecting reacceleration. I believe the valuation is commensurate with the reacceleration story, as I expect a topline inflection of 11.8% in FY26E as integration activity and the adoption of Instant Funding increase. While net debt has increased to $332mn and a $220mn convertible matures in early 2026, RPAY’s 14% post-SBC FCF yield and $250mn revolver offer a buffer of liquidity.

At 2.47x EV/Sales, the stock trades at a 57% discount to its long-term mean and one standard deviation below historical norms, levels that have historically led to attractive forward returns. I intentionally price my multiple below peers and the historical median (4.21x) for the near-term refinancing overhang, but the accelerating fundamental outlook with an active strategic process and historically low valuation support my Strong Buy rating and 120% implied upside.

Downside Risks to My Thesis

Despite my analytical upside, there are material risks that could invalidate my bullish thesis and valuation target. Chief among these are:

Client churn continues: Recent results showed Gross Profit declined 4%–5% y/y, with client attrition (partly due to industry consolidation) weighing on both Consumer Payments and overall revenue far more than I modeled. If churn accelerates or new signings and process integrations do not offset attrition, I could miss my FY26E reacceleration thesis for revenues and margins.

Macro pressures deepen amid slowing organic growth: With organic Gross Profit growth decelerating and macro impacts in the automotive and receivables management verticals remaining acute, a deeper or prolonged downturn would introduce downside to my top-line forecast and resulting EV/Sales-derived PT. Investors should monitor quarterly Gross Profit trends, the pace of new client onboarding and any further negative revisions to guidance.

If client churn remains elevated or revenue growth persists below a single-digit to low-double-digit exit rate, I would need to re-examine my Strong Buy thesis and PT. In sum, despite my constructive view, materially higher client churn and a protracted end-market macro downturn are the most credible risks to my recovery call given the deeply discounted multiples currently assigned.

The Bottom Line

I believe Repay Holdings represents a deeply mispriced, high-conviction opportunity at current levels, with accelerating product adoption and integration velocities set to provide for a sharp re-rating as bottom-up catalysts seemingly underappreciated are baked into results. Conviction is built upon a valuation discount that is well beyond historical norms and forward growth rates, with enterprise sales momentum and operating leverage setting the stage for a highly visible revenue inflection by FY26. For the patient investors, I view the current dislocation provides for a uniquely attractive entry point – watch for evidence of onboarding traction and integration velocity for signals of outsized returns.

data.title: Talen Energy: The Nuclear Energy Advantage
data.description:
Nuclear energy stocks are on the up and up, and Talen Energy (NASDAQ:TLN) is no exception. The power producer, which gets almost 21% of its energy from the source, is up by 33% since I last checked on it in November 2024.

What sets TLN apart is that its 43% rise year-to-date [YTD] is ahead even of other nuclear energy utilities like Constellation Energy (CEG) and Vistra (VTR), puts its rise into perspective (see chart below). This calls for a closer look at the stock to assess what's making it tick and what's next for it in both the short and long term.

The Amazon advantage fuels TLN...

Besides the recent policy support to nuclear energy as a whole due, TLN has benefited from news of an expansion in its partnership with Amazon (AMZN). This expansion is expected in the following two key ways:

  • The power purchase agreement [PPA] targets provision of 1,920 megawatts of nuclear power up to 2042, with the possibility of extension. The delivery can be accelerated, however, resulting in the provision by 2032.
  • The two companies are exploring development of small modular reactors [SMRs], which is also the sector's recent focus in a bid to ramp up nuclear energy production.

On the day of this news release, TLN was up by almost 8%. This isn't the first time that the stock has seen a significant jump on news of its affiliation with Amazon. In March last year, when the partnership was initiated with the sale of its Cumulus Data Centre Campus to Amazon Web Services [AWS], the stock jumped even more, by ~13% in a single day.

There's a good reason for it, too. There has been a flurry of partnerships between nuclear energy companies and big tech in the past year, owing to projected high-energy demand from data centres. Talen Energy's peers like Vistra and Constellation Energy have also signed deals with Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta Platforms (META).

... despite challenges ahead

It's worth pointing out that these partnerships move forward despite two very obvious challenges. The first is regulatory hurdles, which Talen Energy and Amazon have faced since last year. As it happens, the now AWS data centre is co-located to Susquehanna nuclear power plant. This plan will be operated by Talen Energy and provided electricity to the centre.

Regulators have objected to a direct supply of power, however. They believe it can come at the cost of public good, as this arrangement will direct a big chunk of electricity generated to a single customer. Talen has appealed the decision, and while the outcome might still be in favour of the company, the situation still highlights that challenges can come up again.

The next challenge is more fundamental. And that is - would data centres even need as much energy as is currently projected? The question remains after the arrival of DeepSeek's AI models on the scene in late January, with their promise of far smaller energy consumption than that required for traditional models like ChatGPT.

Expectedly, nuclear energy stocks saw a sharp drop as a result. While the episode appears all but forgotten right now, developments on energy requirements can't be ruled out now.

The stock has run up too much

The bullishness on the sector, and the stock, despite the challenges, has resulted in a significant run-up in market multiples. TLN's forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio has now risen to 46.07x, compared to the 28.5x level for 2025 the last I checked.

Also, while at that time its forward P/E was slightly lower than that for CEG at 30x, the tables have now turned. CEG trades at 33.6x for the current year, which is much lower than that for TLN.

The stock's forward GAAP P/E is even higher. The company projects a net income in the range of USD 205-325 million for this year. At the midpoint of the range, this results in a P/E of 50.4x.

Long-term attractiveness persists, though

Purely on the basis of the market multiples, it's possible that the stock can see some pullback on negative news flow. However, TLN is a stock to consider for the long-term since the recovery in nuclear energy, which is driving it, is still in a nascent stage.

Considering this, TLN looks dirt-cheap, with its forward P/E for 2030 at 4.2x. By comparison, CEG trades at 18.3x its earnings estimates for 2030 and VST is at 13.1x.

The risks

The stock isn't without its risks, though. The big one being the overwhelming contribution of fossil fuels to its total energy production at around 80%. These sources are still going strong too, with a 20% year-on-year (YoY) increase in power generation from these sources in the first quarter (Q1 2025).

On its own, there's nothing about this fact that creates a risk for the stock. It only emerges when seen in conjunction with TLN's recent rally due to the optimism around nuclear energy. If the nuclear energy potential doesn't get realised to the extent hoped, TLN's prospects can suffer. And this can happen, for example, if the energy demand indeed isn't what it's projected to be now or SMRs don't live up to their promise.

The next risk is financial. The company reported a net loss of USD 135 million during Q1 2025, a decline of USD 429 million from Q1 2024, when it reported a profit. Talen Energy explains this is because of "the absence of the gain on the sale of the AWS Data Campus, unrealized losses in the nuclear facility decommission trust, and lower realised hedge gains due to higher settled PJM West Hub on-peak prices as a result of colder than normal weather."

To be fair, if these heads' impact is removed from the numbers for Q1 2025, the company would have reported a net income of USD 57 million, which is marginally higher than the corresponding USD 55 million for Q1 2024 with these impacts excluded too.

But that's not enough. The latest results have set it back. On average, the company would have to clock USD 100 million in net profits in each of the remaining three quarters of 2025 to reach the midpoint of its guidance range.

Let me put this into context. Removing the impact of gains on sale of assets from the company's 2024 income, the figure reduces to just USD 114 million, which is an average of USD 28.5 million per quarter. This means, in each of the remaining quarters of 2025, a 3.5x YoY increase in net income is required to achieve the target, which appears doubtful.

What next?

Considering the risk to financial forecasts for 2025 along with the elevated market multiples for now, it's clear that TLN isn't a short-term buy. It's possible that the momentum for the sector can continue to carry the stock forward, but there isn't a fundamental basis for that to happen right now.

The long term, however, is a completely different story. Nuclear Energy's rapid recovery makes Talen Energy one among a handful of companies that are in the right place at the right time. With much policy support along with big tech's growing interest in the space, the company's nuclear energy advantage can hold it in very good stead in the years to come. Moreover, the financial estimates for the long term also reflect well on it. There is a risk of nuclear energy not meeting its promise, but on the whole, things look more positive than not. From this perspective, I'm retaining a Buy rating on TLN.

data.title: Summer Breeze, And Markets Feel Fine
data.description:
If you had to put a human face to the performance of financial markets so far in 2025, to whom would that face bear a likeness? Ten weeks ago, a likely answer might have been Donald Trump, whose authorship of the most radical tariff ideas for the US since the Depression-era Smoot-Hawley Act was sending the stock and bond markets into alternating paroxysms of fear and relief. Six weeks ago, the answer might have been Scott Bessent, the Treasury Secretary, whose measured comments and apparent ability to navigate a steady course through the crazy tweets and policy reversals provided a balm to soothe Wall Street’s frayed nerves.

What, Me Worry?

Our choice for the Face of 2025, though, is neither Bessent nor Trump, nor Jay Powell nor anyone else in the business of financial policymaking. It is Alfred E. Neuman, the iconic avatar of Mad Magazine, a media publication so old that it evokes fond childhood memories even for those of us now in our sixties. The self-satisfied, gap-toothed grin of Mr. Neuman, beaming out from the cover as we eagerly tore into a new issue replete with the Spy versus Spy feature and inane parodies of whatever the hit movie of the day was, bore the message “What, me worry?”

And that seems to be the Zeitgeist of markets in 2025. Everything matters in the world, from old wars (Ukraine) to new wars (Iran), from gutting scientific research grants to flirting with ever-higher debt and deficits, all while pushing the pedal to the metal in our haste to reach the holy grail of artificial general intelligence (AGI) without having any idea what awaits us when (if) we reach that particular Shangri-la. But here in the land of the Dow Jones Industrial Average and the Barclays US Aggregate Bond Index, it’s summertime and the living is easy. Stocks have now recovered substantially all they lost in the wake of the tariff announcements – the S&P 500 is trading right around its last record high, set on February 19, as we write this, while the yield on the 10-year Treasury note is ambling along around 0.25 percent below where it was when the year began.

Too Big to Fail?

Are we surprised at the extent to which calm has returned to markets, given the extreme tempests of April? A bit, yes, but remember that one of our top-level theses in the annual outlook we published back in January was our expectation that markets would act as a guardrail of sorts against the most outlandish ideas of the new administration finding their way into law. Once investors figured out that this dynamic hadn’t changed from what they remembered of the first Trump administration, it was all well and back to the brandy. The assumption baked into this assessment is that markets are too big to fail, and no administration – especially one that considers ultra-wealthy finance types as a core constituent of its political base – is going to let them fail.

And not just with regard to stocks and bonds. Just this morning, we had something of a guardrail-imposed walk-back in reaction to events in the currency market. The US dollar dropped like a stone after a media report made the rounds suggesting that the administration might formally nominate a new Fed chair before the end of this summer, which conjured up the notion of a “shadow Fed” skulking behind Jay Powell for months before his term ends in May next year, casting aspersions on policy decisions not in line with the administration’s preference for steep and immediate interest rate cuts, and tarnishing the Fed’s credibility. The White House promptly put out a statement that no such decision was “imminent,” which was enough to claw back some of the losses the greenback had suffered against the euro and other key currencies.

So the first half of the year will end with not much more than a whimper. Will our luck hold in the second half? Maybe – but while the summer breezes might be gentle now, we are heading into that time of the year when the “low-vol, high-vol” dynamic can be at play. In other words, low trading volume with the resulting potential for high volatility. As we noted in our commentary last week, the coming weeks could bring some unwelcome macro surprises in the form of higher inflation, as the tariff effect finally shows up further down the production value chain, and lower growth as dampened consumer sentiment turns into lower spending. Events in the real world are not going to stop pushing and shoving at markets, and there is always a non-zero probability that one thing or another will penetrate that preternatural calm we have been seeing of late

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Uber Vs. Lyft: Why I Buy Uber And Not The Other
data.description:
At the beginning of my analysis of Lyft (NASDAQ:LYFT), it seemed like a very interesting thesis, with interesting triggers, strategic positioning, and a valuation substantially cheaper than Uber (NYSE:UBER). And although Uber also has an attractive valuation, if the triggers are the same, perhaps it makes sense to capture the bigger discount in Lyft, right?

And as I mentioned in my last analysis about a month and a half ago, the margin of safety for UBER stock has shrunk, and this could generate an opportunity cost if LYFT were much better, so it is worth at least analyzing both companies to see which one makes sense at current valuations.

Well, actually, I don't think so. Upon delving a little deeper into the analysis, it seems to me that the premium on Uber makes a lot of sense, especially in the long term. For investors who, like me, want to ride long-term trends, Uber offers greater security and better positioning in various sectors, and with that, is much less risky.

Therefore, I own and plan to increase my exposure to Uber, while avoiding Lyft. For rating purposes, I reiterate my buy rating on Uber and see a hold rating for Lyft, signaling greater neutrality since it is relatively less interesting than Uber, but it is still a solid company capable of producing attractive returns.

Lyft's Positioning in Relation to Uber

Lyft positions itself with a certain brand differentiation from Uber, more focused on a community environment, more “friendly,” more concerned with drivers, and so on. This is reflected in this investor presentation, where there are several slides dedicated to drivers information and showing that the company cares about its entire ecosystem and carries out various initiatives.

This is obviously positive for sustainability, as it is essential to ensure the satisfaction of all stakeholders. This includes assistance to drivers and metrics such as take-rate, and the image below shows that Lyft is doing a good job in this regard.

But although I see this as a positive and a differentiator, it is somewhat intangible. It is not exactly a huge moat, but rather something that can impact the perception of some user niches and increase the level of loyalty, but it is not possible to quantify how much this can generate in terms of market share, recurring revenue, and the like. What can be said is that, little by little, Lyft has achieved a market share (around 30%) that is already quite reasonable, and this is due to good strategy execution, brand positioning, price differentiation, and the like.

One debatable point is that although this different positioning does exist, it is noted that only 30% of Lyft drivers use this platform exclusively, as the chart below suggests. What this implies is that, whether by choice or necessity, drivers use other platforms such as Uber, and in my view, trends indicate that this is due to Uber's network effect (a strong moat).

What really sets this thesis apart from Uber's thesis today is a different focus. While Uber is a global company, Lyft is more focused on the US and Canada and is beginning to expand into Europe. This means less complexity in costs and regulations, as well as higher quality revenue in the sense of less fluctuation with exchange rates. Dealing with possible restrictions, fines, and other legal disputes is already difficult in one region, imagine in several countries as is the case with Uber.

Revenue diversification is also quite different. Uber has revenue not only from mobility but also from freight and delivery, and the margins of the latter two are lower, with freight still operating at a loss.

There are pros and cons to this diversification. Although diversification seems like a strictly positive thing, it is actually also a loss of focus. Uber could have a team 100% focused on ride-hailing and expansion, but chose to expand its TAM even further with new related initiatives. My take is that Uber already has the scale to make this a positive thing, and since mobility is a business with ever-changing trends (such as AVs), having this diversification ends up mitigating some of the uncertainties.

Financials: Uber Vs. Lyft

Lyft has already achieved some very interesting figures, such as a market share of ~30%, as I mentioned earlier. Gross bookings were $4.1 billion in the last quarter, representing 13% YoY growth. Uber's consolidated gross bookings are over $42 billion, but restricting the comparison to mobility gross bookings alone, the volume is $21 billion, which is not 10x higher, but still 5x higher.

This greater scale and efficiency mean that Uber's adjusted EBITDA margin is 8.3% in this segment, while Lyft's is 2.6%. Both are adjusted for stock-based compensation, which is a very positive point. Lyft's free cash flow chart is also quite impressive. In the last 12 months, FCF was $919 million, which is incredible for a company with a market cap of $6.7 billion.

But the problem is that net income was $60 million in the same period.

So, even though it seems that Lyft is on a similar path to Uber, there is still a long way to go before this profit becomes real. SBC is still a very relevant detractor. While Lyft's SBC represents 5% of revenue, Uber's is around 3.8%, and as Uber's revenue has scaled up significantly and brought other efficiency gains, profitability is much higher.

I'm not saying that Lyft won't get there, but the difference is notable. It's like comparing Inter (INTR) to Nu Holdings (NU). Inter is an excellent thesis (and I even prefer it when compared to Nu Holdings), but Nu Holdings is already much more advanced and deserves a premium for having gotten there, while Inter is still proving itself.

As for growth, Uber has led by a significant margin in recent years, but Lyft is currently posting better numbers.

The outlook, on the other hand, points to very similar numbers. While Lyft estimates a CAGR of 15% for gross bookings from 2024 to 2027 and an EBITDA expansion of 4%, Uber points to a “mid to high teens CAGR” and high 30s% to 40s% CAGR for adj. EBITDA, for the 3-year outlook starting in 2024.

Valuation And Why I Think Uber Is Better

Lyft's valuation, as mentioned, is better than Uber's. The forward price-to-earnings ratio is 13x, and the price-to-OCF ratio is 6.7x. These multiples should give the valuation a very good rating, right? Some other metrics, such as EV-to-sales, are even better, below 1x.

But even so, LYFT receives a B- grade in valuation. And the reason is precisely the GAAP price-to-earnings ratio. Disregarding adjustments such as SBC, which dilutes the shareholder, the forward price-to-earnings ratio is still 65x. This shows that even though the company is generating cash, to generate substantial shareholder value, it will need more scale and more time to mature the business.

Meanwhile, UBER has metrics such as a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 24x non-GAAP and 31x GAAP and a price-to-OCF ratio of 23x. These multiples are much less compelling than those of LYFT, but the quality compensates for this.

For the 65x forward earnings to make sense, the company needs to actually achieve accelerated growth in gross bookings and EBITDA margin. As for Uber, to justify its valuation, it also needs to continue delivering, but note that Uber already delivers. Uber is already the leader in its sector (by far). Uber already has a much higher scale. Uber has a very high network effect and still manages to generate up-sell and cross-sell, with its subscription, more travel modes, and related offers such as Eats. Furthermore, even if this seems to make less sense, Uber's consolidated gross bookings, which are already 10x higher than Lyft's, grew more in the last quarter, with 18% YoY growth.

As with Lyft, there are also opportunities such as AVs and partnerships, and there are still efficiency gains to be explored, all of which should be reflected in the financials. In fact, Uber already has an outlook of adjusted EBITDA growing in the high 30s% to 40s%, with high cash conversion.

In my last article on Uber, in which I downgraded it from Strong Buy to Buy due to the lower margin of safety, I still showed that it was impossible to find an IRR of ~10% when the stock is priced around $90 and in a moderate scenario. Note that even with the 10% gains over the last 5 days, Uber stock is still at a very similar valuation, meaning that this 10% total return is still very feasible.

The point is that this 10% is a proxy. Actually, no long-term valuation model aims to accurately predict how a company will perform, since companies and industries change all the time. If the company performs better, consolidates the sector even further, expands to more regions, offers more services, improves its revenue mix, and so on, that 10% could turn into 11%, 12%, or unlock better short-term upside. But all of this depends on execution, moats, and other related factors.

That is why, in the long term, I believe that Uber's qualities that stand out from Lyft's need a relevant premium, since the conviction that the company will deliver positive results and may present positive surprises is better. On the other hand, as consolidated as Lyft may be, in a market consolidation or industry disruption (and this is one of Uber's risks along with competition and regulation), this is the company that will suffer the most.

Upside Risk For The Underdog

As much as I believe that Uber can offer more diversification and higher quality at a fair price, there are still positive points for Lyft. If I had to sum it up in one sentence, it would be that the valuation is low, and the potential is high (even though the uncertainty is not low).

The chart below shows that management is doing a good job. Revenue is growing at a much faster pace, gaining margin and greatly accelerating free cash flow in recent quarters.

Another point that I find very interesting is that Lyft's greater focus on mobility does not mean that it is standing still and only exploring this traditional market. One example was the acquisition of FREENOW, which expands the company's TAM and adds opportunities such as capturing the growth of taxi hailing in Europe, in addition to, of course, being able to capture synergies over time.

Although I believe that with its scale, Uber will be able to benefit more from the AV trend than Lyft, Lyft also has a strong presence in this sub-segment. In addition to partnerships, the company also owns Flexdrive, which provides fleet management, which is also complementary to the emergence of self-driving cars. Still on the subject of autonomous driving, this was one of the reasons for UBER's recent gains, with the company expanding its partnership with Waymo to reach Atlanta.

In other words, with a low valuation, if these opportunities and the company's maturation continue to translate into revenue growth of more than 10% for a few years, and with decent margin expansion, with such low multiples, the company should indeed be able to unlock considerable upside, and in the short and medium term perhaps even greater than Uber's upside, since Uber's growth and quality are better priced.

The Bottom Line

This seems to me to be a classic case where, in the long term, it is better to have an excellent company at a fair price than the opposite. Lyft, even with an equally interesting narrative, is riskier. Thinking in terms of decades, which of the two companies will have the best chance of capturing the growth of the mobility, delivery, AV, and any other markets that may emerge? In my view, the answer seems clear. Uber has a well-known brand globally and a very relevant scale to be able to venture into optionalities, etc.

For these reasons, my rating for Uber is a buy, due to the quality of the stock and a valuation that still allows for a total return close to double digits next year. Meanwhile, for Lyft, my rating will be neutral (“hold”), since with greater uncertainties, it is more of a play to surf potential re-ratings (which may even be interesting depending on the level of asymmetry that LYFT stock is at) than actually buy & hold.

data.title: Global Medical REIT: Dividend Cut, CEO Changed, And Likely Undervalued
data.description:
Global Medical REIT (NYSE:GMRE) offers quite an attractive way to get leverage on rate cuts, with the potential for a very nice recovery in case the Fed cuts the rates enough for them to not feel high pressure from near-term refinancing. On top of that, the seemingly low valuation, a high dividend yield even after a recent cut and the stock trading at a generous discount to equity make me rate GMRE a Buy now.

Introduction & Financials

Global Medical is a REIT focused on medical clinics and inpatient rehabilitation facilities, with over 191 buildings spread across the US, with most of them specializing in Multi-Specialty, IRF and Ophthalmology. Most of their rent comes from triple-net (55%) and absolute-net (36%) leases, resulting in overall low costs and high predictability for GMRE.

The company has recently reaffirmed its AFFO guidance of $60.85 million at the midpoint, which is below last year’s level of ~$63.4 million last year following some debt repayments. This also doesn’t include the CEO’s succession obligations, which can further lower the AFFO this year. Still, we are talking about ~$60 million on a $450 million market cap, meaning a Price/AFFO Ratio of ~7.5, which is definitely solid.

Financially, based on the company’s latest earnings report, we see an overall good position, with plenty of assets to cover their liabilities - most of which are represented by their drawn credit facility. What’s left is $538 million in equity, which is actually below their $450 million market cap, meaning that their equity is currently trading at a ~16.5% discount today. However, note that this is based on the estimated value of their properties, which could be higher or lower, depending on the buyer and context of selling them (for example, if a company really wants some of their assets, they could be willing to overpay - while in times when the company is desperate they could be willing to sell at 5-10-20% discounts).

Also on the balance sheet, we can see their preferred stock (GMRE.PR.A) and its attributed equity, which comes with a 7.5% yield (7.45% at current price) and has a liquidation preference of $25 per share. I see this as a solid option for anyone who likes the company itself, but would rather have a 7.5% yield with nearly no risk (since the balance sheet is great, and they have a higher priority in case of a bankruptcy) instead of the volatile stock, which is subject to dividend cuts (as you’ll see below) and much more market reaction.

Risks & Opportunities

A key risk here that the market is probably very careful about given the price is the fact that their largest maturity - $350 million term loan A - is due in May 2026 (right when the Fed’s chair’s term should end). Now, the debt itself will likely be refinanced, as they usually do - but the bigger issue is the interest. This loan came with a 2.8% interest rate, which is very low given the current environment. It may not look like a big issue, but if they refinance those $350 million at - let’s say - 5.5%, that’s $19.25 million in interest compared to $9.8 million, so roughly $9.45 million in interest being added, lowering this year’s expected AFFO to $51.4 million from $60.85 million, meaning a very significant >15.5% drop! This could even bring another dividend cut next year, as the new dividend requires about $47.8 million, but they still have a couple of hundred million available in their credit facility which they can use for other costs to free up cash for the dividend. Still, interest should normalize in the long term around lower ratios following rate cuts, but it would definitely bring an even more negative reaction from the market.

At the same time, both a risk and an opportunity could come from their strategy to focus on secondary markets, which aren’t always the highest-quality. This can mean that their assets may be tougher to sell in case they really need it, but there is likely better potential in these markets than the over-saturated and highly competitive ones, especially if they focus on bedroom communities (i.e. towns/areas where people live, but from which they commute to their job). So, this is an overall higher risk but higher potential approach, although if we think about it from the very long-term, it’s likely an overall plus unless we get into short-term issues like a recession. Still, as a new CEO was announced a few days ago, we’ll see if this strategy continues. For that, I’ll wait for the Q2 report and earnings Q&A, where we should get more details about his vision for GMRE’s future.

Also, like many other REITs, Global Medical could benefit from interest rate cuts in several ways (some being anticipated even as early as July). The main goal that central banks target with rate cuts is to stimulate the economy by making borrowing cheaper and increasing disposable income. For GMRE and many other companies, this translates directly into cheaper debt and refinancing opportunities (especially since they need to refinance) for both the company and its operators. As I mentioned in other analyses in the sector, this is especially important for healthcare operators, who can greatly benefit from reduced financing costs since many of them relied on the zero-rate environment for daily operations, especially since many are now trying to recover from the pandemic’s effects. Also, with more disposable income available in the economy, there's potential for increased demand for their tenants' medical services as budgets would allow it, which is a thing, especially in the US where they aren’t free.

On top of the direct impact on the company’s debt, lower rates also lead to a drop in treasury yields, which can lead to a more favorable environment for dividend stocks and especially high-yield REITs like GMRE. As bond yields decrease, the market will need to “risk up” away from the treasuries if it wants higher returns. This can translate into higher demand, especially for the lower-risk stocks, which I think GMRE can be considered given their resilience and balance sheet.

Also, being a relatively tiny company at an around $450 million market cap, there is potential to see other companies buy them, especially after rate cuts since that would make debt cheaper and even encourage M&A, as has been the case ~10 years ago with near-zero rates for several other companies, such as Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) and Medical Properties Trust (MPW). This potential is even higher as the buyer could probably secure them below the value of an equity if they bought today, potentially winning from better contracts or favourable refinancing, generating even tens of millions of dollars in synergies and cost savings.

Dividends

Regarding the dividend, as it was kind of expectable already, Global Medical REIT has recently announced a very significant ~29% quarterly dividend cut (from $0.21 to $0.15), which should fit their refreshed strategy, providing some flexibility to focus on growth opportunities and strengthen the balance sheet. However, looking at the almost absent impact on the stock price, this seems like it was definitely priced in.

Still, even now, we are looking at a dividend yield, which beats other companies in the industry like Omega Healthcare Investors - which I recently analysed and called a Buy - LTC Properties (LTC) or Sila Realty Trust (SILA).

Valuation

For a valuation of Global Medical REIT, I’ll go with $60 million in AFFO (slightly under their 2025 guidance), projecting 4% annual growth for the first 5 years and 3% growth for the following 5 (to account for some more accretive acquisitions and investments), applying a 1% terminal growth rate. I’ll use a 6.5% discount rate that reflects the cost of capital in the healthcare REIT sector but also accounts for rate cuts - slightly under market estimates. This gets us to an estimated enterprise value of approximately $1.23 billion. Subtracting ~$657.9 million in net debt gives an equity value ~$574.67 million, and dividing by ~66.88 million shares outstanding gets to an intrinsic value of ~$8.59 per share, which is ~27.5% above the current price.

Conclusions

Overall, this is a solid company that probably has one final step to take before a proper recovery - next year’s debt maturity. Still, even with that, given the market’s current expectations, I believe GMRE is a Buy now, trading well below its equity and paying a very nice dividend even after the cut. The risks remain, however, especially if they get unfavourable refinancing and are forced to cut the dividend again next year, so rate cuts will be a very important factor for this investment.

data.title: Why Sportradar Can Keep Rallying
data.description:
Sportradar (NASDAQ:SRAD) brings back memories. The profile here — an outstanding business trading at big multiples — is reminiscent of so many software and tech plays in the pre-pandemic, pre-AI world. Like those names (Salesforce (CRM) being the paradigm), SRAD has posted impressive growth, with consistent annual revenue growth of 20% or more, and Adjusted EBITDA jumping 33% in each of the last two years.

But, like those names, that growth seems priced in or close: based on 2025 guidance, SRAD is trading at well past 20x Adjusted EBITDA and a mid-40s multiple on free cash flow, with both multiples deflated somewhat by the exclusion of share-based compensation.

The right answer last decade with high-multiple software plays was to pay up for a quality business; both software and tech more broadly posted impressive performance. For SRAD this decade, the answer seems to be the same — particularly because valuation is not nearly as aggressive as it first appears.

Valuation First

It bears emphasizing up front: SRAD stock is not cheap. Even looking at the three-year growth framework released at April's Investor Day, it looks like the sharp rally of late (shares are +144% over the last year) has priced in expected performance.

The outlook for 2027 projects annualized revenue growth of "at least" 15% over the next three years, with revenue moving to about $2 billion. (As a note, Sportradar reports in euros, but for convenience's sake we'll convert to dollars at the current 1.16 USD/EUR exchange rate.) Adjusted EBITDA margins should expand ~700 basis points to 27%, putting the metric at $528 million. Free cash flow is projected to more than double to $319 million.

At Thursday's close of $27.14, Sportradar has a market cap of $8.7 billion, and an enterprise value of $8.22 billion. (Net cash includes $125 million in payments from the IMG Arena "acquisition" announced in March; market cap includes the conversion of Class B shares into Class A shares at a 10:1 ratio.) And so already SRAD trades at 15.6x 2027 Adjusted EBITDA and 25.8x EV/free cash flow.

In other words, to see double-digit annualized returns over the next 30 months, SRAD needs to exit 2027 with trailing multiples in the range of 20x EBITDA and 30x-plus free cash flow. Those are big multiples in general, and particularly in the context of a stock that until recently hadn't really garnered investor trust since its initial public offering in September 2021:

From a high level, there's definitely a worry that a suddenly hot broad market (the NASDAQ 100 is up 31% in less than three months) has led SRAD stock to disconnect from its fundamentals somewhat. At the very least, there's a sense that the easy money has been made.

The Fundamental Case For Sportradar Stock

But that high-level analysis misses a few key points that suggest SRAD's valuation is more constructive than it might appear.

First, the outlook does not include any contribution from the IMG Arena deal. This was an unusual transaction, to put it mildly (which is why we put "acquisition" in quotes above). Sportradar is getting paid by Endeavor (which was taken private by Silver Lake in March) to take over IMG Arena's sports rights, which include three tennis majors, the PGA Tour, and other properties. Silver Lake didn't want the expense of those rights deals, and with IMG well behind Sportradar and Genius Sports (GENI), likely worried it wouldn't be able to monetize that asset properly.

Founder and chief executive officer Carsten Koerl has said that, pro forma for the deal, revenue growth this year would be in the high-20 percentage point range. That suggests ~140 million EUR (~US$160M) in revenue in the deal; given guidance for "accretive" margins, IMG is probably adding $30 million-plus in annual Adjusted EBITDA. Even without any growth from those rights, that added profit drops the 2027 multiple by about a turn to below 15x. Assuming a 15% effective tax rate, free cash flow rises by about 10%, putting the 2027E EV/FCF multiple at 23x.

Those admittedly are relatively small changes, but the second driver is the possibility of Sportradar exceeding its outlook. Echoing comments from Koerl, chief financial officer Craig Felenstein said at Sportradar's Investor Day in April that "there is significant upside" to the outlook from new markets. That could be new states opening in the U.S. (even if progress in the two prizes, California and Texas, looks completely stalled at the moment) or from new countries (per the Q1 2025 call, Sportradar is focusing on Japan, India, Thailand, and Sri Lanka).

Third, the use of high multiples based on management projections seems worrisome: executives might see 15% (or better) growth, but investing based on company projections is a good way to wind up overpaying. But in this case, Sportradar seems to have earned at least some level of investor confidence. Since being founded a quarter-century ago (on about 200,000 EUR of invested capital), Sportradar has simply been a powerhouse:

As Citizens JMP pointed out in a note earlier this year, Sportradar has topped consensus EBITDA estimates for eleven straight quarters. This simply has been a business that keeps accomplishing what its executives set out to do; there's no guarantee that will continue, but it's easier here to take guidance at face value than it might be for many other companies.

The Long-Term Argument For SRAD

Admittedly, given where valuations currently sit, it's possible Sportradar exceeds its 2027 outlook yet SRAD stock still posts relatively small returns. Profit/cash flow growth here can be offset by multiple compression.

But there is a solid case that investors still will pay up for shares two to three years from now because there is an attractive set of long-term catalysts for the business. Overall market growth should continue: Sportradar expects 10% annualized growth over the five-year period ending in 2029, including 17% in North America. Whatever the actual figure turns out to be (and, as we'll discuss, it's possible to have some skepticism toward the US projections), the overall market should be positive. In Europe, even amid the 2008-09 financial crisis, online sports betting revenue continued to rise.

The mix within sports betting is another tailwind. Over time, bettors generally gravitate to betting more in-play than pre-game; Koerl said at the Investor Day that international customers saw 70-80% of their handle (the amount of money bet) on live bets, while the figure in the U.S. is about 33%. Per the CEO, every point of growth in the penetration of live bets in the U.S. is worth about 6 million EUR (US$7 million) — and that revenue drops to EBITDA at huge margins.

More broadly, shifts within the industry boost revenue as customers add on services beyond basic pre-match analysis:

Live betting is better for Sportradar. The managed trading services operation (which is already doing ~35 billion EUR a year in handle) is better still, and there are ways to build out that business. The Alpha Odds products allows sportsbooks to set lines based on their own liquidity; given Sportradar's reach, it is looking at ways to use the aggregated number of bets it runs to provide hedging opportunities to individual sportsbooks to minimize volatility. (Hold has worked against U.S. sportsbooks of late, for both the National Football League (NFL) regular season and playoffs and the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) basketball tournament.)

Artificial intelligence is another driver. Sportradar already uses AI to collect match/game data (it also has ~20,000 "journalists" who chart the games in person). Conversational AI can make placing a bet easier for novices, and also create content that can attract bettors.

And it's worth returning to the parallels here to 2010s software plays, highlighted in a recent Macquarie note that Sportradar is a "Rule of 40" business (EBITDA margin plus revenue growth is greater than 40 percent). Like those businesses, Sportradar has strong incremental margins, given fixed costs. Those margins are currently 35% and tracking to 40%; Felenstein said at the Investor Day, there was "no reason" they can't get to 50%-plus over time.

To use a term popular last decade, Sportradar quite clearly has a "flywheel" in its model. More data drives better odds, which drives more business, and more data, and better odds, and so on. The sheer amount of data is likely to reshape its media business as well (not immaterial, at ~20% of revenue); it's worth noting that the commissioners of both the National Basketball Association and the National Hockey League attended the company's Investor Day, and there is clear interest in reshaping how games are broadcast and how leagues as a whole are covered.

There's a strong-fixed revenue aspect to the business as well: only about one-third of revenue is variable. Again, there are echoes here from last decade: of the argument for SaaS (software-as-a-service) companies that their contracts are better than first-lien debt: the business will skip an interest payment rather than lose the software (or in this case, data) that is absolutely essential to its business. Acquisitions are another potential avenue for growth; in Brazil, Sportradar has dipped its toe into the iGaming side of the business, and that would be a huge potential opportunity that could be accelerated with a deal.

This simply is an excellent business with a hugely attractive financial model. Yes, the stock looks expensive, but as was the case with so many pre-pandemic tech winners, it should look, and stay, expensive. With EBITDA and cash flow growing 20%-plus, if that's the case, Sportradar stock is going to return 15% even after the rally of the last 20 months (during which time, shares have better than tripled).

What Goes Wrong For Sportradar

That mid-teen annualized return is about what I'd project over the next couple of years; there's enough here for investors to remain optimistic, and that plus underlying growth should drive the stock higher.

Of course, there are a number of risks here as well:

Market growth disappoints. Sportradar is a largely fixed-revenue business — but a lot of the growth comes on the variable revenue side of the business. If the global market isn't growing 10%, that in turn means Sportradar probably doesn't grow as fast as it is modeling.

The core worry here likely is in the U.S., where Sportradar has ~70% market share (rival Genius Sports (GENI) has the contract with the NFL). As noted above, the company is projecting 17% growth in North America, and that figure probably requires at least a couple of new states to come online. There simply aren't that many good candidates, however, with the lieutenant governor of Texas blocking movement there and the long-running morass of interests (business, tribes, politicians, etc.) unable to make progress in California.

Consolidation = lost revenue. The 'sweet spot' for Sportradar is a mid-sized operator: large enough to have material market share, but not so large as to have the capabilities to bring bookmaking in-house. The concern in what have been largely "winner take most" markets is that the large operators will keep taking share from mid-sized and smaller operators. Sportradar will thus lose variable revenue from the mid-sized customers and may lose fixed revenue when smaller customers eventually throw in the towel.

Management was asked about this at the Investor Day; chief commercial officer Eduard Blonk said that most of the consolidation would occur around mid-sized customers, which would be a benefit for Sportradar. Presumably, those tie-ups would make the companies stronger, meaning more market share and thus greater variable revenue share. Those companies likely wouldn't get big enough to threaten broader contracts; a hypothetical merger of Rush Street (RSI) and the online wing of Caesars Entertainment (CZR) still wouldn't be a DraftKings (DKNG) or FanDuel (DUEL).

From here, this looks like the largest risk; the key growth areas for Sportradar are essentially providing outsourced services, and the worry is that the companies that would benefit from outsourced are the ones that will squeezed out of "winner take most" markets. But the history of Europe does suggest that even in a consolidating market there is room for smaller/mid-sized operators. And Sportradar does get some revenue from even the largest players, even if not the same penetration into the business. (As someone who used to bet heavily on middles and arbitrages with player props, it does seem like the lines across different books have narrowed significantly over the past year or so, which in turn suggests that Sportradar and Genius are playing at least some role in setting those lines.)

A spike in rights costs. Sportradar has near-term certainty around its costs; the average rights agreement currently lasts for another six years. But with competition from Genius, and with the leagues potentially having the upper hand in negotiations, there is a worry that the cost of rights will jump as leagues look to take a greater share of the revenue they ostensibly are producing.

This is obviously a difficult risk to quantify, but the data providers will have some negotiating power, particularly if and when their applications of that data materially change the way the sport is covered. I'm personally not quite ready to worry about this, particularly given real risks lie a few years out, but it only takes one bruising negotiation (or loss to Genius) to change the perception here.

Prediction markets? Prediction markets have started offering single-game and futures sports betting, so far, with no apparent pushback from regulators. There is a worry that those markets could take share from sportsbooks — and as peer-to-peer exchanges with no set lines, they have relatively limited if any need for Sportradar services.

For now, this risk doesn't seem quite material; prediction market betting is probably mostly additive to the industry (it allows access from Americans who live in states where sports betting remains illegal). Longer-term, P2P markets have been tried in the US (Sporttrade) and Europe (Betfair) and haven't really taken much share yet. Most casual gamblers probably want a set price, and exchanges face significant liquidity issues around the live betting that is the bread and butter of Sportradar services.

GENI outperforms. Relative to Street consensus, GENI trades at ~17x this year's EBITDA against ~23x for SRAD. There's a case to get the cheaper stock, given that the broader tailwinds are pretty much the same. In fact, depending on timing, GENI has been the bigger play (it's a four-bagger from late 2022 lows).

Owning both is not a terrible strategy, but in this kind of industry, paying up for scale and execution seems wise. Once again, the history of the 2010s matters: there were plenty of opportunities to buy the second-best and/or second-biggest player in a vertical, but the benefits of scale usually made the larger play the better investment.

These risks are real, and in the near-term, gaming sentiment probably play a role in how SRAD trades. But mid- to long-term, this is a company that still has a long growth runway. When offered the chance to pay a high multiple for high growth, investors usually have done well. I don't believe SRAD will be any different.
